checks.a_analysis: The output JSON accurately reflects the content of the source PDF across all seven pages. The meeting agenda, draft minutes header, attendance table, detailed minutes, resignation letter, committee goals, and meeting dates all have a direct basis in the source document. The document title in metadata is an expected AI addition for accessibility. There are no invented section headings or hallucinated body text.

The mailing addresses for Jason P. Steward and Donna Wright found on page 5 are correctly captured in the output JSON without modification. No other contact information (phone numbers, emails, URLs) is present in the source, and none has been added to the output.

All seven pages of the source document are represented in the JSON output. This includes the Agenda (p1), Attendance (p2), Minutes (p3), Roll Call Vote (p4), Resignation Letter (p5), Goals (p6), and Dates (p7).

The attendance table from page 2 and the roll call vote table from page 4 are fully captured. The attendance table is transformed into a series of rows representing the checkbox matrix, but all 23 individuals (plus the empty row shown in the source) are present. The roll call vote table contains all 11 names present in the source.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The detailed meeting minutes in section 3 contain all 11 numbered points from the source. The committee goals in section 6 include all items for both 2021 and 2022. No significant portions of text were omitted during the conversion.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content is highly faithful to the source. It correctly preserves source typos such as 'Tine' instead of 'Tina' in the minutes and '911Board' (no space) in the roll call header, confirming that no arbitrary 'corrections' or meaning-altering changes were made.

checks.a_analysis: The conversion results were reviewed for any accidental duplication of text blocks. While headers and logos appear multiple times, this reflects the repetitive structure of the source document (headers on agenda, minutes, and supplemental pages). The list of committee members appears in both the Attendance table and the Roll Call Vote table, which is faithful to the source. No unintentional content duplication was found.

The document utilizes a logical heading hierarchy. Major page titles are assigned H1 (e.g., 'North Carolina 911 Board' on page 1, 'Standards Committee Goals' on page 6), and subsections like the specific 2021/2022 goal headers are assigned H2. This hierarchy is consistent and appropriate for the document's structure.

The Attendance table on page 2 of the source document consists of a 4-column grid ('Committee Members', 'In Person', 'Via Teams', 'Not Present'). The conversion output (Reading Order 13) has reorganized this into a 2-column 'Field/Value' table, where the column headers and member names are merged into the 'Field' column (e.g., 'Commissioner Marty Cooke - In Person') and the checkbox state is rendered as 'false' in the 'Value' column. This is a significant structural departure from the original grid layout.
checks.b_evidence_source: Table on Page 2 with 4 columns: Committee Members | In Person | Via Teams | Not Present
checks.b_evidence_output: Table at reading_order 13 with 2 columns: Field | Value. Rows merge headers and names: 'Commissioner Marty Cooke - In Person' | 'false'

checks.a_analysis: The reading order follows the page-by-page flow of the source PDF. It correctly sequences the agenda, attendance, minutes, vote table, resignation letter, goals, and meeting dates in the order they appear in the original document.

The source document contains several numbered lists, specifically the 'Meeting Minutes' on page 3 and the 'Standards Committee Goals' on page 6. In the conversion output, these lists are identified as 'unordered' (bulleted) rather than numbered, causing the loss of the original list numbering structure.
checks.b_evidence_source: Numbered list 1-11 on Page 3 (Meeting Minutes) and 1-3 on Page 6 (Goals).

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains no hyperlinks or contact email/web addresses. The output correctly reflects this absence and does not inject any fabricated links.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the visual organization and logical flow of the source PDF. The document sections (Agenda, Attendance, Minutes, Vote Table, Resignation Letter, Goals, and Dates) follow the same sequence as the PDF pages. While the layout is adjusted for HTML (e.g., the checkbox-style attendance grid is flattened into a vertical list), the information remains in the same relative locations and is easy to find. Headings and logos are used to separate sections as in the original.

The tables are correctly rendered and legible. The Agenda table and Roll Call Vote table maintain a clear grid structure. The Attendance table from PDF Page 2, which uses checkboxes in a grid, has been converted into a two-column 'Field/Value' table in HTML. This transformation accurately captures the data (e.g., 'Commissioner Marty Cooke - Via Teams' : 'true' corresponds to the 'x' in the PDF) and is a standard practice for improving web accessibility. There are no overlapping cells or missing borders that hinder comprehension.

The meaningful images, specifically the 'NC DIT 911 Board' logos, are present and rendered correctly at the start of the relevant sections. The 'DRAFT' watermark from the PDF is omitted in the HTML, which is an acceptable and intentional accessibility improvement for decorative elements. The signature on the resignation letter is rendered as text, which preserves the informational content.

There is no duplication of body content in the HTML output. Each section (Agenda, Minutes, Attendance, Votes, etc.) appears exactly once in the main content area. The appearance of headings in both the Table of Contents sidebar and the document body is an intentional accessibility feature and not a defect.
